revision.text:
[[Bild:Orion.jpg|thumb|Aufnahme des Orion]] '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheit [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechnung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. Peeters, Leuven 1995, S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Dekan-Nr. ||[[Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|Hieroglyphen]]Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007.|| Ägyptischer Name ||Übersetzung||Götter/[[Himmelskörper]] |- | 32 | D36:D21 X1:N33 N14-N33:N33 | Aret
''{{unicode|ˁr.t}}'' | ??? | [[Osiris]] (Sternbild Stier) |- | 33 | O20-R8*R8:V31 N14-N33*N33*N33*N33:N33*N33*N33 | Awen Sah
''{{unicode|jwn s3ḫ}}'' | ??? des/von Sah | Osiris (Sternbild Stier/Orion) |- | 34 | D36:Z1 D2:D21 N14-N33 | Remen heri
''{{unicode|rmn ḫr.j}}'' | Arm des Sah | Osiris (Sternbild Orion) |- | 35 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''{{unicode|msḏr rmn ḫr.j}}''

Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9 * [[Otto Neugebauer]], [[Richard-Anthony Parker]]: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts III - Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs -'', Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969 * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Astronomie]] [[Kategorie:Sternbild]]

Es symbolisierte die Gottheit [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechnung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. {{Infobox Ägyptische Gottheit |TITEL = Sah |NAME =S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Sah
''{{Unicode|S3ḥ}}'' }} '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheiten [[Osiris]] sowie [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechnung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt. Vielmehr ist es wahrscheinlich, dass die zugehörigen Sterne eine „zum Trocknen abgezogene Ochsenhaut“ darstellten. Die Bedeutung des schon in frühester Zeit belegten [[Determinativ]]s „sesch“ bezieht sich wohl auf „ausbreiten, ausgebreitet sein“.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 60. == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! ''{{Unicode|S3ḥ}}'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild Orion }} '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheiten [[Osiris]] sowie [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. == Zugehörige Sterne == {{HieroglyphenImText|Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechnung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt. Vielmehr ist es wahrscheinlich, dass die zugehörigen Sterne eine „zum Trocknen abgezogene Ochsenhaut“ darstellten. Die Bedeutung des schon in frühester Zeit belegten [[Determinativ]]s „sesch“ G48 bezieht sich wohl auf „ausbreiten, ausgebreitet sein“ und wurde später zum [[Piktogramm|Kopfemblem]] für den Stern [[Rigel]] (β Orionis) vom Sternbild Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 60.}} == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Dekan-Nr. ||[[Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|Hieroglyphen]]Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. {{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |TITEL = Sah |NAME =S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Sah
''{{Unicode|S3ḥ}}'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild Orion }} '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheiten [[Osiris]] sowie [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. == Zugehörige Sterne == {{HieroglyphenImText|Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt. Vielmehr ist es wahrscheinlich, dass die zugehörigen Sterne eine „zum Trocknen abgezogene Ochsenhaut“ darstellten. Die Bedeutung des schon in frühester Zeit belegten [[Determinativ]]s „sesch“ G48 bezieht sich wohl auf „ausbreiten, ausgebreitet sein“ und wurde später zum [[Piktogramm|Kopfemblem]] für den Hauptstern [[Seba-en-Sah]] ([[Rigel]], β Orionis) vom Sternbild Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 60.}} == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! {{Infobox Hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|ja|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|ja|Sternbild}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|ja|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen}} constellations {{ill|Orion|ja|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion}} and {{ill|Taurus|ja|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|ja|Osiris}} as well as {{ill|Sah|ja|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|ja|Sopdet}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|ja|Sopdu}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|ja|Determinativ}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|ja|Seba-en-Sah}} ({{ill|Rigel|ja|Rigel}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|ja|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|ja|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' {{Infobox Hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|12=draft}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|12=draft}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|12=draft}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|12=draft}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|12=draft}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|12=draft}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|12=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|12=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|12=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|12=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' ''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''

It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|12=draft|12=draft}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft|12=draft}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|12=draft|12=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|12=draft|12=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|12=draft|12=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|12=draft|12=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' Copenhagen 2007. || Egyptian Name || Translation || Gods/{{ill|Celestial Body|de|Himmelskörper|12=draft|12=draft}} |- | 30 | D36:D21 X1:N33 N14-N33:N33 | Aret
''ˁr.t'' | ??? | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Taurus) |- | 31 | D36:Z1 D2:D21 N14-N33 | Remen heri
''rmn ḫr.j'' | Upper arm of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
read: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ear of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | {{ill|Cheret-waret|de|Cheret-waret|12=draft|12=draft}}
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Below the thigh | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- |} == See Also == * {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|12=draft|12=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' {{Infobox Hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|12=draft|12=draft}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|12=draft|12=draft}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|12=draft|12=draft}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|12=draft|12=draft}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|12=draft|12=draft}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|12=draft|12=draft}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|12=draft|12=draft}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|12=draft|12=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|12=draft|12=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|12=draft|12=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|12=draft|12=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' ''rmn ḫr.j'' | Upper arm of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
read: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ear of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | {{ill|Cheret-waret|de|Cheret-waret|12=draft|12=draft}}
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Below the thigh | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- |} == See Also == * {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' revision.comment: 19 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from de:Sah (Sternbild) up to rev 157748127
revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |TITEL = Sah |NAME =S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild Orion }} '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheiten [[Osiris]] sowie [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. == Zugehörige Sterne == {{HieroglyphenImText|Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt. Vielmehr ist es wahrscheinlich, dass die zugehörigen Sterne eine „zum Trocknen abgezogene Ochsenhaut“ darstellten. Die Bedeutung des schon in frühester Zeit belegten [[Determinativ]]s „sesch“ G48 bezieht sich wohl auf „ausbreiten, ausgebreitet sein“ und wurde später zum [[Piktogramm|Kopfemblem]] für den Hauptstern [[Seba-en-Sah]] ([[Rigel]], β Orionis) vom Sternbild Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 60.}} == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Dekan-Nr. ||[[Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|Hieroglyphen]]Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' ''rmn ḫr.j'' | Oberarm des Sah | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah

''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Der unterhalb des Oberschenkels | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Orion) |- |} == Siehe auch == * [[Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. * [[Otto Neugebauer]], [[Richard-Anthony Parker]]: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts.'' Band III: ''Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' revision.text:
{{Infobox Hieroglyphen Zweispaltig |TITEL = Sah |NAME =S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |BILD1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Sternbild Orion }} '''Sah''' war die [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] Bezeichnung eines [[Sternbild]]es, das aus Teilen der [[Astronomie|astronomischen]] Sternbilder [[Orion (Sternbild)|Orion]] und [[Stier (Sternbild)|Stier]] bestand. Es symbolisierte die Gottheiten [[Osiris]] sowie [[Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Sah]], den Gemahl der [[Sopdet]] und Vater des [[Sopdu]]. == Zugehörige Sterne == {{HieroglyphenImText|Aufgrund neuer Erkenntnisse ist es unwahrscheinlich, dass es sich bei dem Sternbild Sah um die alleinige Entsprechung des modernen Sternbildes Orion handelt. Vielmehr ist es wahrscheinlich, dass die zugehörigen Sterne eine „zum Trocknen abgezogene Ochsenhaut“ darstellten. Die Bedeutung des schon in frühester Zeit belegten [[Determinativ]]s „sesch“ G48 bezieht sich wohl auf „ausbreiten, ausgebreitet sein“ und wurde später zum [[Piktogramm|Kopfemblem]] für den Hauptstern [[Seba-en-Sah]] ([[Rigel]], β Orionis) vom Sternbild Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 60.}} == Hintergrund == Das Sternbild Sah umfasste mindestens vier [[Dekade (Altägypten)|Dekane]]. Aus den [[heliakisch]]en Aufgangsdaten ergibt sich im Vergleich zum Sternbild Orion ein flächenmäßig größeres Sternbild, weshalb auch Teile des Sternbildes Stier mit einzubeziehen sind.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. S. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Dekan-Nr. ||[[Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|Hieroglyphen]]Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' Kopenhagen 2007.|| Ägyptischer Name ||Übersetzung||Götter/[[Himmelskörper]] |- | 30 | D36:D21 X1:N33 N14-N33:N33 | Aret
''ˁr.t'' | ??? | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Stier) |- | 31 | D36:Z1 D2:D21 N14-N33 | Remen heri
''rmn ḫr.j'' | Oberarm des Sah | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
lies: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ohr des Sah | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | [[Cheret-waret]]
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Der unterhalb des Oberschenkels | Osiris/Sah (Sternbild Orion) |- |} == Siehe auch == * [[Nutbuch]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. * [[Otto Neugebauer]], [[Richard-Anthony Parker]]: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts.'' Band III: ''Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Sternbild in der altägyptischen Astronomie]]

{{Infobox hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|qq=draft}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|qq=draft}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|qq=draft}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|qq=draft}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|qq=draft}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|qq=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|qq=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|qq=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|qq=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' Copenhagen 2007. || Egyptian Name || Translation || Gods/{{ill|Celestial Body|de|Himmelskörper|qq=draft}} |- | 30 | D36:D21 X1:N33 N14-N33:N33 | Aret
''ˁr.t'' | ??? | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Taurus) |- | 31 | D36:Z1 D2:D21 N14-N33 | Remen heri
''rmn ḫr.j'' | Upper arm of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
read: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ear of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | {{ill|Cheret-waret|de|Cheret-waret|qq=draft}}
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Below the thigh | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- |} == See Also == * {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|qq=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. * {{ill|Otto Neugebauer|de|Otto Neugebauer|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Richard-Anthony Parker|de|Richard-Anthony Parker|qq=draft}}: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts.'' Volume III: ''Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (among others), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == References == == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Constellation in Ancient Egyptian Astronomy]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sah (constellation)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sah (constellation)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[de:Sah_(Sternbild)_]] [[sn:Sah (constellation)]] [[sn:ah]] [[sn: failed]] {{translated page|de|Sah (Sternbild) |version=157748127|comment=Imported full de history}}

Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. {{Infobox hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|qq=draft}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|qq=draft}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|qq=draft}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|qq=draft}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|qq=draft}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|qq=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|qq=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|qq=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|qq=draft}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' Copenhagen 2007. || Egyptian Name || Translation || Gods/{{ill|Celestial Body|de|Himmelskörper|qq=draft}} |- | 30 | D36:D21 X1:N33 N14-N33:N33 | Aret
''ˁr.t'' | ??? | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Taurus) |- | 31 | D36:Z1 D2:D21 N14-N33 | Remen heri
''rmn ḫr.j'' | Upper arm of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
read: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ear of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | {{ill|Cheret-waret|de|Cheret-waret|qq=draft}}
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Below the thigh | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- |} == See Also == * {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|qq=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|qq=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. * {{ill|Otto Neugebauer|de|Otto Neugebauer|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Richard-Anthony Parker|de|Richard-Anthony Parker|qq=draft}}: ''Egyptian Astronomical Texts.'' Volume III: ''Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|qq=draft}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (among others), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == References == == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Constellation in Ancient Egyptian Astronomy]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:Translated pages]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sah (constellation)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sah (constellation)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[de:Sah_(Sternbild)_]] [[sn:Sah (constellation)]] [[sn:ah]] [[sn: failed]] {{translated page|de|Sah (Sternbild) |version=157748127|comment=Imported full de history}}

''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|qq=draft}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|qq=draft}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|qq=draft}}. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|qq=draft}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|qq=draft}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|qq=draft}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! {{Infobox hieroglyphs Two-Column |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constellation|qq=}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Astronomy|qq=}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orion|qq=}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taurus|qq=}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osiris|qq=}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sah|qq=}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet|qq=}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdu|qq=}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Determinative|qq=}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rigel|qq=}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|lt=ancient Egyptian|lt_ja=altägyptische|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egypt|qq=}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|de|Sternbild|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Constellation|qq=}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|de|Astronomie|lt=astronomical|lt_ja=astronomischen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Astronomy|qq=}} constellations {{ill|Orion|de|Orion (Sternbild)|lt=Orion|lt_ja=Orion|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orion|qq=}} and {{ill|Taurus|de|Stier (Sternbild)|lt=Taurus|lt_ja=Stier|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taurus|qq=}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osiris|qq=}} as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sah|qq=}}, the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdet|qq=}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|de|Sopdu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sopdu|qq=}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|de|Determinativ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Determinative|qq=}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}} ({{ill|Rigel|de|Rigel|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rigel|qq=}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan (Ancient Egypt)|de|Dekade (Altägypten)|lt=decans|lt_ja=Dekane|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan (Ancient Egypt)|qq=}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian Hieroglyphs|de|Ägyptische Hieroglyphen|lt=hieroglyphs|lt_ja=Hieroglyphen|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian Hieroglyphs|qq=}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] term for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] that consisted of parts of the [[:en:Astronomy|astronomical]] constellations [[:en:Orion (constellation)|Orion]] and [[:en:Taurus (constellation)|Taurus]]. It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sah|qq=}}, the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative [[:en:Determinative|Determinative]] “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}} ([[:en:Rigel|Rigel]], β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four [[:en:Decan|decans]]. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] term for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] that consisted of parts of the [[:en:Astronomy|astronomical]] constellations [[:en:Orion (constellation)|Orion]] and [[:en:Taurus (constellation)|Taurus]]. It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as {{ill|Sah|de|Sah (Ägyptische Mythologie)|lt=Sah|lt_ja=Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sah|qq=}}, the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative [[:en:Determinative|Determinative]] “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}} ([[:en:Rigel|Rigel]], β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four [[:en:Decan|decans]]. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] term for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] that consisted of parts of the [[:en:Astronomy|astronomical]] constellations [[:en:Orion (constellation)|Orion]] and [[:en:Taurus (constellation)|Taurus]]. It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative [[:en:Determinative|Determinative]] “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}} ([[:en:Rigel|Rigel]], β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four [[:en:Decan|decans]]. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] term for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] that consisted of parts of the [[:en:Astronomy|astronomical]] constellations [[:en:Orion (constellation)|Orion]] and [[:en:Taurus (constellation)|Taurus]]. It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative [[:en:Determinative|Determinative]] “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}} ([[:en:Rigel|Rigel]], β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four [[:en:Decan|decans]]. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! ''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the [[:en:Ancient Egypt|ancient Egyptian]] term for a [[:en:Constellation|Constellation]] that consisted of parts of the [[:en:Astronomy|astronomical]] constellations [[:en:Orion (constellation)|Orion]] and [[:en:Taurus (constellation)|Taurus]]. It symbolized the deities [[:en:Osiris|Osiris]] as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of [[:en:Sopdet|Sopdet]] and the father of [[:en:Sopdu|Sopdu]]. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative [[:en:Determinative|Determinative]] “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}} ([[:en:Rigel|Rigel]], β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four [[:en:Decan|decans]]. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=ancient Egyptian}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|en|Astronomy|WD=Q333|lt=astronomical}} constellations {{ill|Orion (constellation)|en|Orion (constellation)|WD=Q8860|lt=Orion}} and {{ill|Taurus (constellation)|en|Taurus (constellation)|WD=Q10570|lt=Taurus}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris}} as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|en|Sopdu|WD=Q1195229|lt=Sopdu}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|en|Determinative|WD=Q1201172|lt=Determinative}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}} ({{ill|Rigel|en|Rigel|WD=Q12126|lt=Rigel}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=decans}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian hieroglyphs|en|Egyptian hieroglyphs|WD=Q132659|lt=hieroglyphs}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' ''rmn ḫr.j'' | Upper arm of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 32 | D18:D36 W12:D21 N14-N33*N33:N33 | Mesdscher Sah
''msḏr rmn ḫr.j''
read: ''msḏr s3ḫ'' | Ear of Sah | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- | 33 | W12-D21:X1 D56-D21:X1 N14 | {{ill|Cheret-waret|de|Cheret-waret|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cheret-waret|qq=|WD=Q1070217}}
''ẖr.t wˁrt'' | Below the thigh | Osiris/Sah (Constellation Orion) |- |} == See Also == * {{ill|Book of Nut|en|Book of Nut|WD=Q2005559|lt=Book of Nut}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|WD=Q1080802|lt=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Volume III: ''Decans, Planets, Constellations and Zodiacs.'' Brown University Press, Rhode Island 1969. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qq=|WD=Q2643704}}: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (among others), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. == References == == References == {{translated page|de|Sah (Sternbild) |version=157748127|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Sah_(Sternbild)_]] [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] {{wikidata link|Q2211961}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Constellations in Ancient Egyptian Astronomy]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

[[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] [[de:Sah (Sternbild)]] {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITLE = Sah |NAME = S29-Aa18-N29-I5-N14 |NAME-EXPLANATION = Sah
''S3ḥ'' |IMAGE1 = Orion-Sternkarte.png |IMAGE1-WIDTH = 300px |IMAGE1-DESCRIPTION = Constellation Orion }} '''Sah''' was the {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|WD=Q11768|lt=ancient Egyptian}} term for a {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|WD=Q8928|lt=Constellation}} that consisted of parts of the {{ill|Astronomy|en|Astronomy|WD=Q333|lt=astronomical}} constellations {{ill|Orion (constellation)|en|Orion (constellation)|WD=Q8860|lt=Orion}} and {{ill|Taurus (constellation)|en|Taurus (constellation)|WD=Q10570|lt=Taurus}}. It symbolized the deities {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|WD=Q46491|lt=Osiris}} as well as [[:en:Sah|Sah]], the consort of {{ill|Sopdet|en|Sopdet|WD=Q930167|lt=Sopdet}} and the father of {{ill|Sopdu|en|Sopdu|WD=Q1195229|lt=Sopdu}}. == Associated Stars == {{HieroglyphenImText|Due to new findings it is unlikely that the constellation Sah corresponds exclusively to the modern constellation Orion. Rather, it is probable that the associated stars represented a “dried ox-hide”. The meaning of the determinative {{ill|Determinative|en|Determinative|WD=Q1201172|lt=Determinative}} “sesch” G48 likely refers to “to spread, spread out” and was later used as the pictographic head emblem for the principal star {{ill|Seba-en-Sah|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}} ({{ill|Rigel|en|Rigel|WD=Q12126|lt=Rigel}}, β Orionis) of the constellation Sah.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 60.}} == Background == The constellation Sah comprised at least four {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|WD=Q5248738|lt=decans}}. From the heliacal rising data it appears that, in comparison to the constellation Orion, it covered a larger area; this is why parts of the constellation Taurus were also included.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren''. p. 64. {| class="wikitable sortable" |- class="hintergrundfarbe8" ! Decan No. || {{ill|Egyptian hieroglyphs|en|Egyptian hieroglyphs|WD=Q132659|lt=hieroglyphs}}Alexandra von Lieven: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch.''
